perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the term "Customer" includes all Affiliates listed in Exhibit A, attached hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the term "Customer" might include some Affiliates listed in Exhibit A, attached hereto.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "includes all Affiliates" to "might include some Affiliates" introduces ambiguity and contradicts standard legal interpretations, as the original clause clearly defines the scope of "Customer" while the modified version makes this uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement emphasizes the need for reasonable certainty in contract terms. The original text is definite, while the modified text injects uncertainty regarding which Affiliates are included, potentially making the contract unenforceable due to vagueness.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation introduces ambiguity by changing "includes all Affiliates" to "might include some Affiliates." This contradicts the need for certainty in contract terms, as the modified text injects uncertainty regarding which Affiliates are included. The original text clearly defines "Customer," whereas the modified version introduces vagueness. The Law Explanation states this potential uncertainty could make the contract unenforceable. While the provided law snippets are not helpful, the Law Explanation indicates the Restatement's emphasis on reasonable certainty, which the changed text violates.

perturbation.original_text: Metavante warrants that it will provide all Services in a commercially reasonable manner in material conformance with the applicable Documentation (the "Performance Warranty"). Where the parties have agreed upon Service Levels for any aspect of Metavante's performance, such Service Levels shall apply in lieu of the Performance Warranty.
perturbation.changed_text: Metavante warrants that it will attempt to provide some Services in what it determines to be a reasonable manner in general conformance with some of the Documentation (the "Performance Warranty"). Where the parties have vaguely discussed potential Service Levels for any aspect of Metavante's performance, such vague discussions might apply instead of some portion of the Performance Warranty.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces multiple uncertainties and ambiguities. The original clause provides a clear 'Performance Warranty' with agreed 'Service Levels' acting as a substitute where applicable. The modified version weakens the warranty by changing 'will provide all Services' to 'attempt to provide some Services', making the standard of care subjective by changing 'commercially reasonable manner' to 'what it determines to be a reasonable manner,' and diluting the conformance requirement by changing 'material conformance with the applicable Documentation' to 'general conformance with some of the Documentation.' Furthermore, the change to Service Levels introduces even more ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: UCC § 2-313 - Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample
perturbation.law_explanation: This change undermines the concept of express warranties under the UCC. The original warranty, while limited, was relatively clear. The modifications create vagueness and uncertainty as to the scope and nature of the warranty, potentially rendering it unenforceable under UCC principles, which require clear and definite affirmations or promises to create an express warranty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the express warranty, creating a contradiction with UCC principles as described in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original text commits to providing 'all Services' in a 'commercially reasonable manner' and in 'material conformance' with documentation, establishing a clear standard. The modified text, however, changes 'will provide all Services' to 'attempt to provide some Services' and weakens the standard of care and conformance, thus undermining the definiteness required for an express warranty under UCC § 2-313. The introduction of subjective determination ('what it determines to be a reasonable manner') further erodes the clarity and enforceability of the warranty, contradicting the requirement that affirmations or promises become part of the basis of the bargain.

perturbation.original_text: Customer shall maintain adequate records for at least ten (10) Business Days, including backup on magnetic tape or other electronic media where transactions are being transmitted to Metavante, from which reconstruction of lost or damaged files or data can be made. Customer assumes all responsibility and liability for any loss or damage resulting from failure to maintain such records.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer should try to keep some records for a short period, perhaps a few days, possibly using some kind of storage. Customer is probably responsible for any loss if they don't do this.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states a clear requirement to maintain 'adequate' records for 'at least ten (10) Business Days.' The modified version weakens this obligation by using vague language like 'should try,' 'some records,' 'short period, perhaps a few days,' and 'possibly using some kind of storage.' This introduces uncertainty about the Customer's obligations and the enforceability of the clause. The term 'adequate records' and a minimum period of retention is a legal standard that would be used in determining responsibility for losses.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the principle of mitigation of damages in contract law. The original clause aligns with this principle by requiring the Customer to maintain records that would allow for the reconstruction of lost data, thereby mitigating potential damages. The modified text, with its weakened language, undermines this duty and could be interpreted as absolving the Customer of the responsibility to take reasonable steps to minimize losses.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the customer's obligation to maintain adequate records for a specified period, replacing it with vague suggestions. The original text requires maintaining 'adequate records for at least ten (10) Business Days,' while the changed text suggests the customer 'should try to keep some records for a short period, perhaps a few days.' This introduces ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of the clause, because 'adequate records' and a minimum time period are legal standards. Although there are no scraped snippets to reinforce this point, the perturbation explanation clearly states this contradiction, noting that this deviation from a clear, enforceable requirement introduces uncertainty and weakens the original obligation to keep adequate records. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, as the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.
